title: Restoration of Ensemble Equivalence by Quantum Fluctuations
abstract: We study the thermodynamic phase diagram of a one-dimensional quantum spin chain subjected to both mean-field and nearest-neighbor interactions, and to a transverse magnetic field $h$. The purpose is to determine the effect of the quantum fluctuations, due to the transverse field, on the phase diagram, in particular with respect to the occurrence of ensemble inequivalence. We denote our model as a quantum Nagle-Kardar model. To perform the calculation of the canonical partition function, we show that, due to the presence of the mean-field term, in the thermodynamic limit one can use the Hubbard-Stratonovich transformation in spite of the non-commutativity of the different operators appearing in the Hamiltonian, and we adopt a procedure of successive approximations that lead to the determination of the phase diagram thanks to a scaling property of the phase transition lines. The results show that the ensemble inequivalence, present in the classical Nagle-Kardar model, is removed above a threshold value $h_c$ for the transverse field. For $h$ larger than $h_c$ the phase diagram exhibits only second-order phase transition lines, implying therefore restoration of ensemble equivalence.
summary: This paper explores the impact of a transverse magnetic field on the thermodynamic phase diagram of a quantum spin chain with long-range and short-range interactions. It specifically addresses the phenomenon of ensemble inequivalence, which is a hallmark of certain classical long-range systems. The authors demonstrate that sufficiently strong quantum fluctuations can suppress first-order transitions, thereby restoring ensemble equivalence. By using a p-blocking approximation and a rescaling technique, they provide a way to map the phase transitions of the full model using computationally tractable finite-size blocks.
main_problem: The study investigates whether quantum fluctuations, induced by a transverse magnetic field, can restore ensemble equivalence in a long-range interacting system that exhibits ensemble inequivalence in its classical limit.
main_result: The authors find that ensemble inequivalence present in the classical Nagle-Kardat model is removed above a threshold transverse field value hc, where phase transitions become exclusively second-order.
method: The researchers use a Hubbard-Stratonovich transformation combined with a p-blocking approximation and a rescaling procedure to calculate the partition function and determine the phase diagram.
model_or_system: A one-dimensional quantum spin chain, known as the quantum Nagle-Kardar model, featuring mean-field (all-to-all) ferromagnetic coupling, nearest-neighbor interactions, and a transverse magnetic field.
key_observables: Average magnetization per spin and the nature of phase transition lines (first-order vs. second-order).
important_parameters: Transverse field strength (h), mean-field coupling (J), nearest-neighbor coupling (K), temperature (T), and the blocking parameter (p).
main_assumptions: The study assumes that quantum fluctuations play a role similar to thermal fluctuations regarding equilibrium order and relies on a scaling property to extrapolate from finite p-blocks to the thermodynamic limit.
figures_summary: Figure 1 illustrates the clustering/p-blocking method; Figure 2 shows the (K, T) phase diagram with first and second-order lines and tricritical points; Figure 3 shows the clustering of phase diagrams for different p values; Figure 6 demonstrates the successful collapse of rescaled curves.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the quantum Nagle-Kardar model, details the Hubbard-Stratonovich and p-blocking methodologies, presents the numerical results for various p-blocks and h values, demonstrates the scaling/rescaling success, and concludes with the identification of the critical field for restoring ensemble equivalence.
why_interesting: This paper is interesting for many-body dynamics researchers because it demonstrates how quantum-induced fluctuations can fundamentally alter the thermodynamic stability and ensemble properties of long-range interacting systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
